mnemonics.title: PAGE FAULT HANDLING STEPS
mnemonics.content: TRI TO HANDLE PAGE FAULT: 1) TRAP to OS 2) Identify the virtual address 3) CHECK if address is valid 4) FIND a free frame 5) LOAD page from disk 6) UPDATE page table 7) RESTART instruction.

mnemonics.title: PAGE REPLACEMENT ALGORITHMS
mnemonics.content: FIFO: FIRST IN FIRST OUT (OLDEST PAGE OUT) LRU: LEAST RECENTLY USED (OLDEST IN TIME) OPT: OPTIMAL (FUTURE KNOWLEDGE)

mnemonics.title: FRAGMENTATION TYPES
mnemonics.content: INTERNAL FRAGMENTATION: INSIDE allocated blocks (wasted space) EXTERNAL FRAGMENTATION: OUTSIDE (scattered free blocks)

mnemonics.title: VIRTUAL MEMORY BENEFITS
mnemonics.content: VIRTUE: VIRTUAL MEMORY GIVES PROCESSES IllUSION of larger memory than physically available. Benefits: 1) More processes 2) Protection 3) Sharing 4) Relocation

acronyms.usage: Page replacement algorithm that evicts the oldest page in memory
Page replacement algorithm that evicts the least recently accessed page
Hardware cache that stores recent virtual-to-physical address translations
Address generated by CPU in a virtual memory system
Hardware unit that translates virtual addresses to physical addresses

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a grid (physical memory) divided into equal squares (frames). Another grid (virtual memory) with same-sized squares (pages). A mapping (page table) connects corresponding squares between them. When a page is needed, the OS finds an empty square in physical memory and draws a line from virtual to physical.

Think of a library (OS) with books (pages) in stacks (disk) and on desks (memory). When a student (process) needs a book not on a desk, they request it. The librarian (OS) finds an empty desk space, brings the book from stacks, puts it on the desk, and tells the student where it is.

Visualize a factory (system) where workers (processes) constantly need tools (pages) but the storage (frames) is too small. Workers spend more time fetching and returning tools than actually working (executing). The more they fetch, the less work gets done.

Think of a large office building with different departments (segments): HR, Engineering, Sales. Each department has its own floor (segment) with specific rooms (offsets). To find someone, you need the department name (segment number) and room number (offset).

analogies.analogy: Virtual memory is like a library's book reservation system. You can request books that aren't currently on the shelf (in memory). The librarian (OS) brings them from the back storage (disk) when you need them, even though the shelf (physical memory) is full.

The page table is like a GPS navigation system. Your car (CPU) knows where you want to go (virtual address), and GPS (MMU/page table) translates it to actual road directions (physical address) to reach your destination.

Swapping is like moving students between a small classroom (physical memory) and a larger study hall (disk). When the classroom is full, some students move to the study hall to make room, and they switch places when needed.

The working set is like the books currently open on your desk while studying. You keep only the relevant ones nearby (in memory) and keep the rest in your bag (disk). As you study different subjects, you swap books on your desk.

Copy-on-write is like two students sharing lecture notes. Initially they use the same notebook. If one needs to write personal notes, they make a photocopy first. This saves paper until actual writing is needed.
